texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane researchers have been aware of the trade-off between selectivity ( α ) and permeability ( P ) since the early days of membrane gas separation technology. Robeson fi rst formulated this trade-off relationship in his in fl uential paper [1]. You can have high selectivity or high permeability, but it is dif fi cult to have both at the same time. Another important and less appreciated membrane parameter is the pressure ratio ( θ ), de fi ned as
texts.text: Membrane researchers have been aware of the trade-off between selectivity ( α ) and permeability ( P ) since the early days of membrane gas separation technology. Robeson fi rst formulated this trade-off relationship in his in fl uential paper [1]. You can have high selectivity or high permeability, but it is dif fi cult to have both at the same time. Another important and less appreciated membrane parameter is the pressure ratio ( θ ), de fi ned as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: θ ¼ p f p p ð 1 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where p f and p p are the feed and permeate pressures on either side of the membrane. The impact of pressure ratio on membrane gas separation processes is the subject of this paper.
texts.text: where p f and p p are the feed and permeate pressures on either side of the membrane. The impact of pressure ratio on membrane gas separation processes is the subject of this paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In many materials research studies, pressure ratio is ignored or treated as a freely adjustable parameter and set at a conveniently high value, where its impact on the separation can be neglected. Unfortunately, in industrial gas separation processes, adjustment of the pressure ratio comes at a signi fi cant price in terms of compressor capital cost and power usage. The cost of the compressor package may be much more than the cost of the membrane unit, and the cost of electricity used to power the compressor is usually the largest operating expense. For these reasons, pressure ratios used in industrial processes are normally below 20, and are typically in the range of 5 -15.
texts.text: In many materials research studies, pressure ratio is ignored or treated as a freely adjustable parameter and set at a conveniently high value, where its impact on the separation can be neglected. Unfortunately, in industrial gas separation processes, adjustment of the pressure ratio comes at a signi fi cant price in terms of compressor capital cost and power usage. The cost of the compressor package may be much more than the cost of the membrane unit, and the cost of electricity used to power the compressor is usually the largest operating expense. For these reasons, pressure ratios used in industrial processes are normally below 20, and are typically in the range of 5 -15.

texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The feed and permeate pressures achievable in most gas separations are limited by economics. As a result, commercial membrane separation processes usually operate with a feed-to-permeate pressure ratio ( θ ) in the range of 5 -15. This limited pressure ratio means that the optimum membrane may not be the one with the highest selectivity; rather, a membrane with lower selectivity, but tailored to the pressure ratio, may result in a more economical process. In other words, when a process is pressure ratio limited, the useful selectivity is also limited. The balance between pressure ratio and selectivity is often ignored by membrane developers. The importance of this balance is illustrated with two applications of current interest: CO2 capture from power plant fl ue gas, and dehydration of azeotropic bioethanol.
texts.text: The feed and permeate pressures achievable in most gas separations are limited by economics. As a result, commercial membrane separation processes usually operate with a feed-to-permeate pressure ratio ( θ ) in the range of 5 -15. This limited pressure ratio means that the optimum membrane may not be the one with the highest selectivity; rather, a membrane with lower selectivity, but tailored to the pressure ratio, may result in a more economical process. In other words, when a process is pressure ratio limited, the useful selectivity is also limited. The balance between pressure ratio and selectivity is often ignored by membrane developers. The importance of this balance is illustrated with two applications of current interest: CO2 capture from power plant fl ue gas, and dehydration of azeotropic bioethanol.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we will use two speci fi c applications to illustrate the importance of pressure ratio and its impact on membrane selection.
texts.text: In this paper, we will use two speci fi c applications to illustrate the importance of pressure ratio and its impact on membrane selection.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The fi rst application is capture of CO2 from power plant fl ue gas. Flue gas from combustion of fossil fuels is produced in enormous volumes and contains 5 -15 mol% CO2 at atmospheric pressure [2,3]. The membrane system must remove 50 -90% of this CO2 in a concentrated stream for sequestration. This is a low pressure separation, so large membrane areas are needed. But the key economic concern is the energy consumed by the compression/vacuum equipment that drives the process. This energy use is directly related to the membrane pressure ratio. A competitive process requires an affordably low pressure ratio, and this determines the preferred membrane selectivity.
texts.text:  The fi rst application is capture of CO2 from power plant fl ue gas. Flue gas from combustion of fossil fuels is produced in enormous volumes and contains 5 -15 mol% CO2 at atmospheric pressure [2,3]. The membrane system must remove 50 -90% of this CO2 in a concentrated stream for sequestration. This is a low pressure separation, so large membrane areas are needed. But the key economic concern is the energy consumed by the compression/vacuum equipment that drives the process. This energy use is directly related to the membrane pressure ratio. A competitive process requires an affordably low pressure ratio, and this determines the preferred membrane selectivity.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The second application is dehydration of azeotropic ethanol/ water mixtures in bioethanol plants. This is the fi nal dehydration step in the production of dry bioethanol and is a large potential market for water-permeable vapor permeation membranes [4]. The objective is to break the azeotrope produced by distillation of the ethanol fermentation broth, reducing the water content from 16 mol% (7 wt%) to 0.76 mol% (0.3 wt%). The permeate vapor is usually cooled and condensed to spontaneously create a vacuum on the permeate side of the membrane, enabling relatively high pressure ratios to be achieved. However, the extreme operating conditions (ethanol/water vapor mixtures at 100 -130 1 C) require expensive membrane module materials, so controlling membrane area is important. We will show that the balance between pressure ratio and selectivity also affects membrane area.
texts.text:  The second application is dehydration of azeotropic ethanol/ water mixtures in bioethanol plants. This is the fi nal dehydration step in the production of dry bioethanol and is a large potential market for water-permeable vapor permeation membranes [4]. The objective is to break the azeotrope produced by distillation of the ethanol fermentation broth, reducing the water content from 16 mol% (7 wt%) to 0.76 mol% (0.3 wt%). The permeate vapor is usually cooled and condensed to spontaneously create a vacuum on the permeate side of the membrane, enabling relatively high pressure ratios to be achieved. However, the extreme operating conditions (ethanol/water vapor mixtures at 100 -130 1 C) require expensive membrane module materials, so controlling membrane area is important. We will show that the balance between pressure ratio and selectivity also affects membrane area.

texts.orig: Membrane gas permeation is quanti fi ed in terms of membrane permeability and selectivity using the solution-diffusion equation:
texts.text: Membrane gas permeation is quanti fi ed in terms of membrane permeability and selectivity using the solution-diffusion equation:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: j i ¼ Pi ℓ ð p f i � p p i Þ ð 2 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where j i is the molar fl ux (cm 3 (STP)/cm 2 s) of component i , ℓ is the membrane thickness, p f i and p p i are the partial vapor pressures of component i on the feed side and permeate side of the membrane, and Pi is the permeability of the membrane material to component i , usually expressed in Barrer (where 1 Barrer ¼ 1 -10 � 10 cm 3 (STP) cm/cm 2 s cmHg). Because the thickness of the selective layer in composite membranes is often dif fi cult to measure, commercial membranes are typically characterized by their partial pressure normalized fl ux ( j i = Δ p i ) or permeance ( Pi = ℓ ), which is expressed in gas permeation units or gpu (where 1 gpu ¼ 1 -10 � 6 cm 3 (STP)/cm 2 s cmHg).
texts.text: where j i is the molar fl ux (cm 3 (STP)/cm 2 s) of component i , ℓ is the membrane thickness, p f i and p p i are the partial vapor pressures of component i on the feed side and permeate side of the membrane, and Pi is the permeability of the membrane material to component i , usually expressed in Barrer (where 1 Barrer ¼ 1 -10 � 10 cm 3 (STP) cm/cm 2 s cmHg). Because the thickness of the selective layer in composite membranes is often dif fi cult to measure, commercial membranes are typically characterized by their partial pressure normalized fl ux ( j i = Δ p i ) or permeance ( Pi = ℓ ), which is expressed in gas permeation units or gpu (where 1 gpu ¼ 1 -10 � 6 cm 3 (STP)/cm 2 s cmHg).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane separation capability, or selectivity, is expressed as the ratio of the permeances or permeabilities of components i and j :
texts.text: The membrane separation capability, or selectivity, is expressed as the ratio of the permeances or permeabilities of components i and j :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: α ij ¼ Pi = ℓ Pj = ℓ ¼ Pi Pj ð 3 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where α ij is the selectivity of the membrane for component i over component j .
texts.text: where α ij is the selectivity of the membrane for component i over component j .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By way of illustrating the importance of pressure ratio, consider the one-stage CO2/N2 separation process shown in Fig. 1. For CO2 to permeate the membrane, the partial pressure of CO2 in the permeate must be less than the partial pressure of CO2 in the feed, so we can write:
texts.text: By way of illustrating the importance of pressure ratio, consider the one-stage CO2/N2 separation process shown in Fig. 1. For CO2 to permeate the membrane, the partial pressure of CO2 in the permeate must be less than the partial pressure of CO2 in the feed, so we can write:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: p p -permeate CO2 concentration ð C p CO2 Þ r p f -feed CO2 concentration ð C f CO2 Þ ð 4 Þ

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: C p CO2 C f CO2 r p f p p ¼ θ ð 5 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: or membrane enrichment r pressure ratio ð θ Þ
texts.text: or membrane enrichment r pressure ratio ð θ Þ

texts.label: text

texts.text: This simple relation can also be written as

texts.orig: C p CO2 θ r C f CO2 ð 7 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the separation shown in Fig. 1, the feed contains 10 mol% CO2. From Eq. (5), this means that, at a pressure ratio of 5, the permeate can contain no more than 50 mol% CO2. Thus, the rest of the permeate must be nitrogen, the slower permeating component. It follows that the membrane area required to permeate a speci fi c volume of CO2 at a speci fi c pressure ratio is determined more by the permeation rate of the slow component (nitrogen) than by that of the fast component (CO2). In the limit that the permeation rate of the slow component drops to zero (an in fi nitely selective membrane), no slow component permeates, so an in fi nite membrane area is required.
texts.text: In the separation shown in Fig. 1, the feed contains 10 mol% CO2. From Eq. (5), this means that, at a pressure ratio of 5, the permeate can contain no more than 50 mol% CO2. Thus, the rest of the permeate must be nitrogen, the slower permeating component. It follows that the membrane area required to permeate a speci fi c volume of CO2 at a speci fi c pressure ratio is determined more by the permeation rate of the slow component (nitrogen) than by that of the fast component (CO2). In the limit that the permeation rate of the slow component drops to zero (an in fi nitely selective membrane), no slow component permeates, so an in fi nite membrane area is required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The impact of membrane selectivity on membrane performance at a particular pressure ratio is illustrated graphically in Fig. 2; two examples are shown here, one is at pressure ratio of 5, and the other is at pressure ratio of 10. The membranes are assumed to have the same CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu, and the selectivity is varied by changing the nitrogen permeance. The feed CO2 concentration is assumed to be 10 mol% and the feed fl ow rate is 1000 m 3 (STP)/h. This plot and others shown later in this paper could have been calculated using analytical solutions derived by Pan and Habgood in the 1970s [5 -7]. However, in this paper, these plots have been generated using differential element membrane code written at MTR and incorporated into a computer process simulation program (ChemCad 6.3, ChemStations, Austin, TX).
texts.text: The impact of membrane selectivity on membrane performance at a particular pressure ratio is illustrated graphically in Fig. 2; two examples are shown here, one is at pressure ratio of 5, and the other is at pressure ratio of 10. The membranes are assumed to have the same CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu, and the selectivity is varied by changing the nitrogen permeance. The feed CO2 concentration is assumed to be 10 mol% and the feed fl ow rate is 1000 m 3 (STP)/h. This plot and others shown later in this paper could have been calculated using analytical solutions derived by Pan and Habgood in the 1970s [5 -7]. However, in this paper, these plots have been generated using differential element membrane code written at MTR and incorporated into a computer process simulation program (ChemCad 6.3, ChemStations, Austin, TX).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First consider the curves in Fig. 2 calculated at a pressure ratio of 5. The plot in Fig. 2(a) shows the permeate CO2 concentration as a function of increasing membrane selectivity. At a selectivity of 1 (no separation), the permeate concentration is the same as the feed. As the selectivity increases, the permeate concentration increases, reaching about 40 mol% CO2 at a selectivity of 40. As the selectivity increases further, the permeate concentration approaches the limiting value of 50 mol% given by Eq. (5). Fig. 2 (b) shows the relative membrane area required to permeate a fi xed amount of CO2 at the same conditions used in Fig. 2(a). This plot has an exponential form where the required membrane area increases rapidly as membrane selectivity increases. It follows that at a pressure ratio of 5 the optimum selectivity under the chosen conditions is between about 20 and 40. A higher selectivity produces a slight increase in permeate CO2 concentration, but at the expense of a very large increase in membrane area (and cost).
texts.text: First consider the curves in Fig. 2 calculated at a pressure ratio of 5. The plot in Fig. 2(a) shows the permeate CO2 concentration as a function of increasing membrane selectivity. At a selectivity of 1 (no separation), the permeate concentration is the same as the feed. As the selectivity increases, the permeate concentration increases, reaching about 40 mol% CO2 at a selectivity of 40. As the selectivity increases further, the permeate concentration approaches the limiting value of 50 mol% given by Eq. (5). Fig. 2 (b) shows the relative membrane area required to permeate a fi xed amount of CO2 at the same conditions used in Fig. 2(a). This plot has an exponential form where the required membrane area increases rapidly as membrane selectivity increases. It follows that at a pressure ratio of 5 the optimum selectivity under the chosen conditions is between about 20 and 40. A higher selectivity produces a slight increase in permeate CO2 concentration, but at the expense of a very large increase in membrane area (and cost).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider now the curves in Fig. 2 calculated at a pressure ratio of 10. As the membrane selectivity increases, the permeate CO2 concentration increases approaching a limiting value of 100 mol% given by Eq. (5). As before, the membrane area increases with increasing selectivity. Comparing the curves for the two pressure ratios, it is clear that increasing the pressure ratio, if economially possible, has a very bene fi cial effect: at the same selectivity and CO2 permeance, a smaller membrane area is needed to produce the same amount of permeate, which has a higher concentration of CO2. However, even at

As before, the membrane area increases with increasing selectivity. Comparing the curves for the two pressure ratios, it is clear that increasing the pressure ratio, if economially possible, has a very bene fi cial effect: at the same selectivity and CO2 permeance, a smaller membrane area is needed to produce the same amount of permeate, which has a higher concentration of CO2. texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 1. Separation of CO2 from nitrogen by a CO2-selective membrane at a pressure ratio of 5 (5 bar feed, 1 bar permeate). The permeate can never contain more than 50 mol% CO2, even when the membrane is in fi nitely selective.
texts.text: Fig. 1. Separation of CO2 from nitrogen by a CO2-selective membrane at a pressure ratio of 5 (5 bar feed, 1 bar permeate). The permeate can never contain more than 50 mol% CO2, even when the membrane is in fi nitely selective.

texts.orig: Fig. 2. The effect of membrane selectivity and pressure ratio on (a) the permeate CO2 concentration and (b) the required membrane area, for the separation process illustrated in Fig. 1. In these calculations, CO2 permeance is fi xed at 1000 gpu and selectivity is varied by changing the N2 permeance. The feed CO2 concentration is 10 mol%, and feed fl ow rate is 1000 m 3 (STP)/h.
texts.text: Fig. 2. The effect of membrane selectivity and pressure ratio on (a) the permeate CO2 concentration and (b) the required membrane area, for the separation process illustrated in Fig. 1. In these calculations, CO2 permeance is fi xed at 1000 gpu and selectivity is varied by changing the N2 permeance. The feed CO2 concentration is 10 mol%, and feed fl ow rate is 1000 m 3 (STP)/h.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3. (a) Permeate CO2 concentration and (b) CO2 fl ux at varying feed gas CO2 concentrations and CO2/nitrogen selectivities. All separations are performed at a pressure ratio of 5 (5 bar feed, 1 bar permeate), with membranes having a CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu, at feed fl ow rate of 1000 m 3 (STP)/h. There is a trade-off between CO2 fl ux and CO2 concentration in the permeate. The membrane with a selectivity of 10 has the highest fl ux, but the lowest CO2 enrichment. The membrane with a selectivity of 1000 has the lowest fl ux, but the highest enrichment.
texts.text: Fig. 3. (a) Permeate CO2 concentration and (b) CO2 fl ux at varying feed gas CO2 concentrations and CO2/nitrogen selectivities. All separations are performed at a pressure ratio of 5 (5 bar feed, 1 bar permeate), with membranes having a CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu, at feed fl ow rate of 1000 m 3 (STP)/h. There is a trade-off between CO2 fl ux and CO2 concentration in the permeate. The membrane with a selectivity of 10 has the highest fl ux, but the lowest CO2 enrichment. The membrane with a selectivity of 1000 has the lowest fl ux, but the highest enrichment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the higher pressure ratio of 10, a tradeoff between membrane area and permeate CO2 concentration remains, with the optimum selectivity now in the range of 40 -100.
texts.text: the higher pressure ratio of 10, a tradeoff between membrane area and permeate CO2 concentration remains, with the optimum selectivity now in the range of 40 -100.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another way to illustrate the impact of pressure ratio on the optimum membrane selectivity is shown in Fig. 3. As before, the calculations are made at a fi xed CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu, a feed fl ow rate of 1000 m 3 (STP)/h, and the selectivity is changed by varying the nitrogen permeance. The pressure ratio is set at 5. The fi gure shows the changes in permeate CO2 concentration and CO2 fl ux as a function of CO2 feed concentration and membrane selectivity. The feed concentration is varied from 0 -30 mol% CO2. A series of curves is shown for membranes with selectivities between 10 and 1000. Good quality polymeric membranes typically have selectivity between 30 and 60 for this gas mixture [2,8], and facilitated transport membranes have been reported with selectivities up to 1000 [9,10], so all of the membranes shown in Fig. 3 are potentially available.
texts.text: Another way to illustrate the impact of pressure ratio on the optimum membrane selectivity is shown in Fig. 3. As before, the calculations are made at a fi xed CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu, a feed fl ow rate of 1000 m 3 (STP)/h, and the selectivity is changed by varying the nitrogen permeance. The pressure ratio is set at 5. The fi gure shows the changes in permeate CO2 concentration and CO2 fl ux as a function of CO2 feed concentration and membrane selectivity. The feed concentration is varied from 0 -30 mol% CO2. A series of curves is shown for membranes with selectivities between 10 and 1000. Good quality polymeric membranes typically have selectivity between 30 and 60 for this gas mixture [2,8], and facilitated transport membranes have been reported with selectivities up to 1000 [9,10], so all of the membranes shown in Fig. 3 are potentially available.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The plots in Fig. 3 are divided into two regions: a pressureratio-limited region and a non-pressure-ratio-limited region. The dividing line between these two regions is taken to be the point at which the permeate concentration can theoretically reach 100 mol % CO2. From Eq. (7), this feed CO2 concentration is then:
texts.text: The plots in Fig. 3 are divided into two regions: a pressureratio-limited region and a non-pressure-ratio-limited region. The dividing line between these two regions is taken to be the point at which the permeate concentration can theoretically reach 100 mol % CO2. From Eq. (7), this feed CO2 concentration is then:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: C f CO2 ¼ C p CO2 θ ¼ 100 5 mol % ¼ 20 mol % ð 8 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Eq. (8), the permeate CO2 concentration cannot reach 100 mol% if the feed CO2 concentration is below 20 mol%, even with an in fi nitely selective membrane; to increase the permeate CO2 concentration, the pressure ratio has to be increased. Hence, at feed concentrations below 20 mol% CO2, the separation is pressure ratio limited.
texts.text: From Eq. (8), the permeate CO2 concentration cannot reach 100 mol% if the feed CO2 concentration is below 20 mol%, even with an in fi nitely selective membrane; to increase the permeate CO2 concentration, the pressure ratio has to be increased. Hence, at feed concentrations below 20 mol% CO2, the separation is pressure ratio limited.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The feed CO2 concentration given by the term 100/ θ mol% is a useful, albeit arbitrary marker of the pressure-ratio-region boundary. However, in the pressure-ratio-limited region, there is still some in fl uence of membrane selectivity on separation, as can be seen from Fig. 3. Likewise, outside the pressure-ratio-limited region, the pressure ratio will still exert some in fl uence on the process performance.
texts.text: The feed CO2 concentration given by the term 100/ θ mol% is a useful, albeit arbitrary marker of the pressure-ratio-region boundary. However, in the pressure-ratio-limited region, there is still some in fl uence of membrane selectivity on separation, as can be seen from Fig. 3. Likewise, outside the pressure-ratio-limited region, the pressure ratio will still exert some in fl uence on the process performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Considering Fig. 3(a) fi rst, at a fi xed feed concentration, for example 10 mol% CO2, the CO2 enrichment in the permeate increases as the membrane selectivity increases. However, the CO2 concentration in the permeate soon reaches the limit set by Eq. (5), represented by the line in the fi gure for an in fi nitely selective membrane. As a result, the impact of high membrane selectivity on membrane performance is less than what might be expected. At low CO2 feed concentrations, the difference in permeate CO2 concentration obtained using a membrane with selectivity of 1000 and one with a selectivity of 50 is small. The bene fi ts of a high selectivity membrane become signi fi cant only at

However, the CO2 concentration in the permeate soon reaches the limit set by Eq. (5), represented by the line in the fi gure for an in fi nitely selective membrane. As a result, the impact of high membrane selectivity on membrane performance is less than what might be expected. At low CO2 feed concentrations, the difference in permeate CO2 concentration obtained using a membrane with selectivity of 1000 and one with a selectivity of 50 is small. The bene fi ts of a high selectivity membrane become signi fi cant only at

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The fl ux data for the same membranes are plotted in Fig. 3(b). All the membranes have the same CO2 permeance, but the fl ux is different at different selectivities. The membrane with a selectivity of 10 has the highest CO2 fl ux. Membranes with higher selectivities have lower CO2 fl uxes, despite the fact that the CO2 permeance is unchanged. In reality, the membrane with a selectivity of 10 would normally be 3- to 10-fold more permeable than the membrane with selectivity of 100. This would make the difference in membrane fl uxes shown in Fig. 3(b) even more extreme than that shown.
texts.text: The fl ux data for the same membranes are plotted in Fig. 3(b). All the membranes have the same CO2 permeance, but the fl ux is different at different selectivities. The membrane with a selectivity of 10 has the highest CO2 fl ux. Membranes with higher selectivities have lower CO2 fl uxes, despite the fact that the CO2 permeance is unchanged. In reality, the membrane with a selectivity of 10 would normally be 3- to 10-fold more permeable than the membrane with selectivity of 100. This would make the difference in membrane fl uxes shown in Fig. 3(b) even more extreme than that shown.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The decrease in CO2 fl ux with increasing selectivity is a result of the need to permeate the slow gas, nitrogen. The effect is most marked in the pressure-ratio-limited region, that is, at feed gas CO2 concentrations below 20 mol%. In this concentration range, a membrane with an in fi nite selectivity has no fl ux and the fl ux of a membrane with a selectivity of 1000 is very small. Practically, this means that using a high selectivity membrane will require a much larger membrane area (and incur much higher cost), without producing much bene fi t in CO2 enrichment.
texts.text: The decrease in CO2 fl ux with increasing selectivity is a result of the need to permeate the slow gas, nitrogen. The effect is most marked in the pressure-ratio-limited region, that is, at feed gas CO2 concentrations below 20 mol%. In this concentration range, a membrane with an in fi nite selectivity has no fl ux and the fl ux of a membrane with a selectivity of 1000 is very small. Practically, this means that using a high selectivity membrane will require a much larger membrane area (and incur much higher cost), without producing much bene fi t in CO2 enrichment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It follows from Fig. 3 that, at a fi xed pressure ratio set by the economics of the process, membranes with different selectivities are preferred at different feed concentrations. In the feed concentration range 0 -10 mol% CO2, a membrane with selectivity between 20 and 50 is probably optimal. A higher selectivity membrane -for example a membrane with selectivity of 100 -might give a slightly higher CO2 permeate concentration, but, because of the lower fl ux, would require more than double the membrane area to perform essentially the same separation. In the feed concentration range 10 -20 mol% CO2, the balance between fl ux and CO2 enrichment is optimized with a membrane selectivity of 50 -100; at 20 -30 mol% CO2, the optimum membrane selectivity is around 100 -200. Only at feed concentrations above about 30 mol% CO2 is the highest possible selectivity desirable (subject to the membrane retaining adequate CO2 permeance). These results suggest that, in an industrial process, membranes of different selectivities might be used in different portions of the plant, depending on the speci fi c concentration of CO2 in the gas stream being processed.
texts.text: It follows from Fig. 3 that, at a fi xed pressure ratio set by the economics of the process, membranes with different selectivities are preferred at different feed concentrations. In the feed concentration range 0 -10 mol% CO2, a membrane with selectivity between 20 and 50 is probably optimal. A higher selectivity membrane -for example a membrane with selectivity of 100 -might give a slightly higher CO2 permeate concentration, but, because of the lower fl ux, would require more than double the membrane area to perform essentially the same separation. In the feed concentration range 10 -20 mol% CO2, the balance between fl ux and CO2 enrichment is optimized with a membrane selectivity of 50 -100; at 20 -30 mol% CO2, the optimum membrane selectivity is around 100 -200. Only at feed concentrations above about 30 mol% CO2 is the highest possible selectivity desirable (subject to the membrane retaining adequate CO2 permeance). These results suggest that, in an industrial process, membranes of different selectivities might be used in different portions of the plant, depending on the speci fi c concentration of CO2 in the gas stream being processed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation performance of the high selectivity membranes in Fig. 3 can be improved by increasing the pressure ratio across the membrane. However, pressure ratios of 20 or 50 -available at the click of a button in a computer simulation -are unrealistic for fl ue gas CO2 capture. A few trial calculations show that the maximum affordable pressure ratio is about 10 -15; beyond this value, the power consumption for compressors and vacuum pumps becomes a large fraction of the total power output of the plant, making the membrane process completely uneconomical [2].
texts.text: The separation performance of the high selectivity membranes in Fig. 3 can be improved by increasing the pressure ratio across the membrane. However, pressure ratios of 20 or 50 -available at the click of a button in a computer simulation -are unrealistic for fl ue gas CO2 capture. A few trial calculations show that the maximum affordable pressure ratio is about 10 -15; beyond this value, the power consumption for compressors and vacuum pumps becomes a large fraction of the total power output of the plant, making the membrane process completely uneconomical [2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another way to approach the fl ue gas CO2 capture problem is to use multi-stage processes. Pressure ratio also impacts these designs. Fig. 4 shows a two-stage separation process designed to reduce the CO2 content of a fl ue gas stream from 10 mol% to 2 mol %. The permeate from the fi rst membrane unit is recompressed and sent to a second unit. The residue from the second unit is recycled to the front of the process. The fi rst membrane produces a residue stream of 2 mol% CO2, achieving a CO2 removal of 85%. But the permeate is only partially concentrated, to 40 -50 mol% CO2. The second membrane concentrates the fi rst permeate gas, yielding a twice-enriched CO2 permeate product stream. The concentration of the second-stage residue stream is set at 10 mol% CO2, the same as the feed to which it is recycled, to avoid process inef fi ciency caused by mixing streams of unequal concentrations.
texts.text: Another way to approach the fl ue gas CO2 capture problem is to use multi-stage processes. Pressure ratio also impacts these designs. Fig. 4 shows a two-stage separation process designed to reduce the CO2 content of a fl ue gas stream from 10 mol% to 2 mol %. The permeate from the fi rst membrane unit is recompressed and sent to a second unit. The residue from the second unit is recycled to the front of the process. The fi rst membrane produces a residue stream of 2 mol% CO2, achieving a CO2 removal of 85%. But the permeate is only partially concentrated, to 40 -50 mol% CO2. The second membrane concentrates the fi rst permeate gas, yielding a twice-enriched CO2 permeate product stream. The concentration of the second-stage residue stream is set at 10 mol% CO2, the same as the feed to which it is recycled, to avoid process inef fi ciency caused by mixing streams of unequal concentrations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two calculations are shown in Fig. 4; one assumes a selectivity of 50, the other a selectivity of 200. The membranes are again assumed to have a CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu. A pressure ratio of 10 is provided by maintaining 1 bar on the feed side and drawing a vacuum of 0.1 bar on the permeate side. Compressing the feed gas to 10 bar, and keeping the permeate at atmospheric pressure, would produce the same pressure ratio and would signi fi cantly reduce the membrane area needed, but at the expense of more power, which is a critical factor in this application [2]. The process shown is sized to treat a fl ue gas containing 20 t/day of CO2 at 10 mol% CO2, about the amount of fl ue gas generated by a coal power plant for each MWe of electricity produced. The low selectivity process uses about 20% more power and produces a less concentrated CO2 than the high selectivity process. On the other hand, the membrane area needed for the low selectivity process is only about 1/3 of that used for the high selectivity option (13,760 m 2 vs. 37,120 m 2 ).
texts.text: Two calculations are shown in Fig. 4; one assumes a selectivity of 50, the other a selectivity of 200. The membranes are again assumed to have a CO2 permeance of 1000 gpu. Compressing the feed gas to 10 bar, and keeping the permeate at atmospheric pressure, would produce the same pressure ratio and would signi fi cantly reduce the membrane area needed, but at the expense of more power, which is a critical factor in this application [2]. The process shown is sized to treat a fl ue gas containing 20 t/day of CO2 at 10 mol% CO2, about the amount of fl ue gas generated by a coal power plant for each MWe of electricity produced. The low selectivity process uses about 20% more power and produces a less concentrated CO2 than the high selectivity process. On the other hand, the membrane area needed for the low selectivity process is only about 1/3 of that used for the high selectivity option (13,760 m 2 vs. 37,120 m 2 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Could the process shown in Fig. 5 be improved by changing the pressure ratio? The results of some calculations to answer this question are given in Fig. 6. This fi gure shows the effect of changing the pressure ratio from 5 to 30. At a pressure ratio of 5, the driving force for separation is low, so the required membrane area is large. The fl ow of recycle gas from the second stage to the fi rst is high, necessitating large vacuum pumps, and the product quality is poor; the fi nal product only contains 74% CO2.
texts.text: Could the process shown in Fig. 5 be improved by changing the pressure ratio? The results of some calculations to answer this question are given in Fig. 6. This fi gure shows the effect of changing the pressure ratio from 5 to 30. At a pressure ratio of 5, the driving force for separation is low, so the required membrane area is large. The fl ow of recycle gas from the second stage to the fi rst is high, necessitating large vacuum pumps, and the product quality is poor; the fi nal product only contains 74% CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increasing the pressure ratio to between 10 and 15 is very bene fi cial. The higher driving force signi fi cantly reduces the membrane area and the size of the vacuum equipment. Simultaneously, the CO2 concentration in the product gas increases to 97%. At pressure ratios above 15, the membrane area continues to decrease and the product gas CO2 concentration continues to increase, but to a slight degree. However, the vacuum pump power consumption ramps up quickly. Since power consumption is a key parameter, the optimum pressure ratio is chosen to be 10, as was used for Fig. 5 calculations. It is worth noting that even though the second stage of the process operates outside the pressure-ratio-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of membranes to dehydrate azeotropic water/ethanol vapor mixtures is illustrated in Fig. 7. In this process, the feed to the membrane unit is the overhead vapor from an ethanol/water distillation column. This vapor has a pressure of about 3 bar at 110 1 C. The permeate pressure is 0.1 bar, set by the condensation temperature achievable with cooling water, and enabling a relatively high pressure ratio of 30 to be achieved. The membranes available for this separation normally have water/ethanol selectivities in the 20 -200 range [11].

The membranes available for this separation normally have water/ethanol selectivities in the 20 -200 range [11].

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 8. (a) Permeate water concentration and (b) water fl ux at varying feed water concentrations, for membranes with different water/ethanol selectivities but constant water permeance of 1000 gpu at a feed fl ow rate of 1000 kg/h. All separations are performed at a pressure ratio of 30 (3 bar feed, 0.1 bar permeate). At this pressure ratio, the process is in the pressure-ratio-limited range at feed water concentrations below 3.3 mol%. There is a trade-off between water fl ux and water concentration in the permeate. The membrane with a selectivity of 10 has the highest fl ux, but the lowest permeate water concentration. The membrane with a selectivity of 1000 has the lowest fl ux, but the highest permeate water concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: selectivity membranes, so the membrane with the highest selectivity available should be used. In contrast, at feed water concentrations below 3.3 mol% the separation is in the pressure-ratiolimited region [from Eq. (8), C f H2O ¼ C p H2O = θ ¼ 100 = 30 mol % ¼ 3 : 3 mol % ]. and the water fl uxes are more affected by membrane selectivity. In this range, membranes with selectivity above 100 have low water fl uxes, necessitating the use of large membrane areas. The higher enrichment obtained with high selectivity membranes will not offset the increased cost for the much higher membrane area. Therefore, at feed concentrations below 3.3 mol%, membranes with selectivities between 20 and 100 are preferred. In the intermediate feed concentration range between 3.3 and 6 mol%, membranes with intermediate selectivities between 100 and 200 are optimal.
texts.text: selectivity membranes, so the membrane with the highest selectivity available should be used. In contrast, at feed water concentrations below 3.3 mol% the separation is in the pressure-ratiolimited region [from Eq. (8), C f H2O ¼ C p H2O = θ ¼ 100 = 30 mol % ¼ 3 : 3 mol % ]. and the water fl uxes are more affected by membrane selectivity. In this range, membranes with selectivity above 100 have low water fl uxes, necessitating the use of large membrane areas. The higher enrichment obtained with high selectivity membranes will not offset the increased cost for the much higher membrane area. Therefore, at feed concentrations below 3.3 mol%, membranes with selectivities between 20 and 100 are preferred. In the intermediate feed concentration range between 3.3 and 6 mol%, membranes with intermediate selectivities between 100 and 200 are optimal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some calculations to illustrate how these considerations affect the design of a water/ethanol separation system are provided in Fig. 9. To reach the target ethanol purity, a two-step process is used. The fi rst step, which operates outside the pressure-ratiolimited region, lowers the water concentration from 16 mol% to 3.3 mol%, producing a water-rich permeate that can be recycled to the distillation column. The second step, which operates within the pressure-ratio-limited region, lowers the water concentration in the ethanol product from 3.3 mol% to 0.76 mol%.
texts.text: Some calculations to illustrate how these considerations affect the design of a water/ethanol separation system are provided in Fig. 9. To reach the target ethanol purity, a two-step process is used. The fi rst step, which operates outside the pressure-ratiolimited region, lowers the water concentration from 16 mol% to 3.3 mol%, producing a water-rich permeate that can be recycled to the distillation column. The second step, which operates within the pressure-ratio-limited region, lowers the water concentration in the ethanol product from 3.3 mol% to 0.76 mol%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although the amount of water that permeates in the fi rst membrane step is much larger than in the second step, the membrane areas required for each step are comparable. Two sets of calculations are shown in Fig. 9. Fig. 9(a) shows the separation obtained when membranes with a water/ethanol selectivity of 20 are used in both steps; Fig. 9(b) shows the comparable calculations with selectivity of 200. The high selectivity membrane is clearly preferred for the fi rst step. Although the required membrane area is a bit larger (194 m 2 vs. 140 m 2 ), the permeate stream contains much more water (90.8 mol% vs. 58.6 mol%) and hence much less ethanol, and is of much smaller volume (72 kg/h vs. 164 kg/h) than when the lower selectivity membrane is used. As a result, ethanol recycle to the distillation column is reduced, improving the overall process ef fi ciency.
texts.text: Although the amount of water that permeates in the fi rst membrane step is much larger than in the second step, the membrane areas required for each step are comparable. Two sets of calculations are shown in Fig. 9. Fig. 9(a) shows the separation obtained when membranes with a water/ethanol selectivity of 20 are used in both steps; Fig. 9(b) shows the comparable calculations with selectivity of 200. The high selectivity membrane is clearly preferred for the fi rst step. Although the required membrane area is a bit larger (194 m 2 vs. 140 m 2 ), the permeate stream contains much more water (90.8 mol% vs. 58.6 mol%) and hence much less ethanol, and is of much smaller volume (72 kg/h vs. 164 kg/h) than when the lower selectivity membrane is used. As a result, ethanol recycle to the distillation column is reduced, improving the overall process ef fi ciency.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The choice of the optimum membrane for the second step is not as clear. The high selectivity membrane produces a signi fi cantly better quality permeate (41.6 mol% H2O vs. 19 mol%), but uses more than three times the membrane area (428 m 2 vs. 124 m 2 ).
texts.text: The choice of the optimum membrane for the second step is not as clear. The high selectivity membrane produces a signi fi cantly better quality permeate (41.6 mol% H2O vs. 19 mol%), but uses more than three times the membrane area (428 m 2 vs. 124 m 2 ).

texts.label: text

The membrane selectivity of the fi rst step is fi xed at 200; the second-step selectivity is varied between 5 and 200. Now both the effect of the second membrane selectivity on the second permeate (recycle stream) quality and the interaction of selectivity with pressure ratio can be linked to the total membrane area requirement.

The membrane selectivity of the fi rst step is fi xed at 200; the second-step selectivity is varied between 5 and 200. Now both the effect of the second membrane selectivity on the second permeate (recycle stream) quality and the interaction of selectivity with pressure ratio can be linked to the total membrane area requirement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Fig. 10 process, the mass and water concentration of the recycle stream vary with the selectivity of the second-step membrane. Since the recycle stream is returned to the feed side of the fi rst membrane separation step, performance of this step is also affected. When the selectivity of the second-step membrane is very low, for example 5, the recycle stream is large and contains a large amount of ethanol that is recirculated through the process. Dehydration of the 1000 kg/h feed stream is still possible, but large membrane areas are required for both the fi rst and second membrane steps.
texts.text: In Fig. 10 process, the mass and water concentration of the recycle stream vary with the selectivity of the second-step membrane. Since the recycle stream is returned to the feed side of the fi rst membrane separation step, performance of this step is also affected. When the selectivity of the second-step membrane is very low, for example 5, the recycle stream is large and contains a large amount of ethanol that is recirculated through the process. Dehydration of the 1000 kg/h feed stream is still possible, but large membrane areas are required for both the fi rst and second membrane steps.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increasing the selectivity of the second-step membrane from 5 to 20 has a very bene fi cial effect. The concentration of water in the second-step permeate stream increases and the volume of the stream decreases. The result is to greatly reduce recycle of ethanol and water through the process. The membrane areas required to perform both separation steps are reduced signi fi cantly.
texts.text: Increasing the selectivity of the second-step membrane from 5 to 20 has a very bene fi cial effect. The concentration of water in the second-step permeate stream increases and the volume of the stream decreases. The result is to greatly reduce recycle of ethanol and water through the process. The membrane areas required to perform both separation steps are reduced signi fi cantly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When the selectivity of the second-step membrane increases to more than 30, the effect of pressure ratio demonstrated in Fig. 8 becomes apparent. The concentration of water in the second-step permeate continues to increase slowly and the volume of the recycle stream continues to decrease slowly, but these improvements are counteracted by a reduction in the transmembrane water fl ux, leading to a sharp increase in the second-step membrane area requirement, which becomes very noticeable at selectivities above about 60.
texts.text: When the selectivity of the second-step membrane increases to more than 30, the effect of pressure ratio demonstrated in Fig. 8 becomes apparent. The concentration of water in the second-step permeate continues to increase slowly and the volume of the recycle stream continues to decrease slowly, but these improvements are counteracted by a reduction in the transmembrane water fl ux, leading to a sharp increase in the second-step membrane area requirement, which becomes very noticeable at selectivities above about 60.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 11 plots the membrane area (for the fi rst step, second step and total) required to perform the separation as a function of second-step membrane selectivity. The membrane area needed has a broad minimum for second-step membrane selectivities between 10 and 40. When the energy cost of re-evaporating the recycle stream is taken into account, the optimum membrane selectivity is likely to be slightly higher, in the range of 30 -50.

The membrane area needed has a broad minimum for second-step membrane selectivities between 10 and 40. When the energy cost of re-evaporating the recycle stream is taken into account, the optimum membrane selectivity is likely to be slightly higher, in the range of 30 -50.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: with different properties in different portions of the plant. The analysis also illustrates the importance of considering the operating conditions of the total process when developing membranes for real-world industrial applications.
texts.text: with different properties in different portions of the plant. The analysis also illustrates the importance of considering the operating conditions of the total process when developing membranes for real-world industrial applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To make useful membranes , you have to understand the process . In many industrial gas separations, process economics limit the pressure ratio that can be used. As a consequence, the optimum membrane may not be the one with the highest selectivity, and membranes with different selectivities may be preferred in different portions of separation plants. High permeance membranes are always good, but the optimum membrane selectivity depends on the process and the operating conditions, particularly the pressure ratio.
texts.text: To make useful membranes , you have to understand the process . In many industrial gas separations, process economics limit the pressure ratio that can be used. As a consequence, the optimum membrane may not be the one with the highest selectivity, and membranes with different selectivities may be preferred in different portions of separation plants. High permeance membranes are always good, but the optimum membrane selectivity depends on the process and the operating conditions, particularly the pressure ratio.
